title: Robust spin-squeezing on quantum networks: the lesson from universality
authors: Andrea Solfanelli, Augusto Smerzi, Peter Zoller, Nicolò Defenu
abstract: We establish the conditions under which scalable spin squeezing can be achieved in interacting spin ensembles embedded in arbitrary, inhomogeneous network geometries. We identify two different forms of squeezing: OAT-like scalable squeezing is governed solely by the universal properties of the interaction graph and is controlled by its spectral dimension. In critical squeezing, on the other hand, the value of the spectral dimension only furnishes the necessary condition for scalable metrological gain, while the sufficient condition requires the model to lie below the symmetry breaking transition. Therefore, in quantum networks, the scaling of the spin-squeezing critical point emerges from a nontrivial interplay between xy-ferromagnetic universality and percolation universality. We apply this general theoretical framework to several experimental scenarios and discuss sharp and experimentally relevant conditions for achieving robust metrological gain on generic inhomogeneous structures, giving a unifying perspective for designing scalable quantum sensors across diverse quantum simulation platforms.
summary: This paper establishes how the geometry and disorder of a quantum network determine the possibility of scalable spin squeezing. It identifies that squeezing can occur via two different mechanisms depending on the network's spectral dimension. The findings offer a guide for engineering robust quantum sensors using platforms like Rydberg arrays or trapped ions.
main_problem: Determining the necessary and sufficient conditions for achieving scalable spin squeezing in interacting spin ensembles embedded in arbitrary, inhomogeneous, and disordered network geometries.
main_result: The study identifies two distinct squeezing regimes (OAT-like and critical) governed by the spectral dimension of the network, and establishes that the scaling of the spin-squeezing critical point arises from the interplay between XY-ferromagnetic and percolation universality.
method: The authors use a generalized rotor/spin-wave theory based on the graph Laplacian, linear spin-wave theory, and the Discrete Truncated Wigner Approximation (DTWA) to analyze the dynamics.
model_or_system: The model is the XXZ Hamiltonian on generic graphs and networks, including site-diluted lattices and engineered geometries, applicable to platforms like trapped ions, Rydberg atom arrays, and ultracold molecules.
key_observables: Spin-squeezing parameter (xi^2), phase sensitivity, transverse variances, and collective magnetization.
important_parameters: Spectral dimension (ds), interaction exponent (alpha), anisotropy (Delta), dilution probability (p), and the percolation threshold (pp).
main_assumptions: The analysis utilizes the rotor/spin-wave approximation and assumes the dominance of isolated dimers near the percolation transition for certain scaling derivations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows different classes of inhomogeneous systems; Figure 2 presents a hierarchy of conditions for metrological advantage; Figure 3 relates spectral dimension to random walk properties; Figure 4 compares spin-wave spectra via exact diagonalization versus graph approximations; Figure 9 shows spin-squeezing dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of squeezing in networks, develops a low-energy theory using the graph Laplacian, identifies two metrological regimes based on spectral dimension, analyzes scaling near the percolation threshold, and applies the framework to experimental platforms.
why_interesting: It provides a unifying theoretical framework for designing scalable quantum sensors by linking quantum metrology to fundamental concepts like graph universality, spectral dimension, and percolation theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic representations of the three classes of inhomogeneous systems considered in this work: (a) long-range interacting lattices with finite filling fraction or site dilution, modeling realistic experimental conditions in a variety of quantum simulation platforms, including trapped ions, Rydberg atom arrays, polar molecules, and solid-state spin systems such as NV centers (Sec. III A 1); (b) engineered graph geometries, realizable for instance in Rydberg atom arrays coupled to optical cavities through the application of spatially modulated fields, enabling programmable interaction networks (Sec. III A 2); (c) systems with spatially correlated disorder, modeling...

Figure 2. Summary of the hierarchy of necessary and suffi- cient conditions to obtain scalable metrological advantage on quantum networks. The first, necessary geometric prerequi- site is the existence of a giant percolating cluster. On top of this geometric condition, two distinct dynamical mechanisms can lead to scalable squeezing depending on the spectral di- mension ds of the graph: for ds > 3, the system lies in the mean-field universality class and exhibits OAT-like squeezing; for 2 < ds < 3, scalable squeezing can still arise due to the existence of the finite temperature transition, see Sec. I C.

Figure 3. Schematic representation of the necessary conditions for scalable spin squeezing. The possibility of scalable metrolog- ical advantage beyond the standard quantum limit is governed by the universality of the spin system on the underlying graph, as encoded by the spectral dimension ds. For ds > 3, the system lies in the mean-field regime, where spin-wave excitations are irrelevant on the optimal squeezing timescale, and the dynamics is dominated by the collective zero mode, leading to OAT-like scalable spin squeezing. In the intermediate regime 2 < ds < 3, interactions beyond mean-field become important; nevertheless, the transience of random walks on the graph enables spontaneous...

Figure 4. Comparison of the spin-wave spectra obtained by exact diagonalization of the real-space quadratic Hamiltonian (23) (dots), and by the regular graph approximation (33) (blue dashed lines), for different dilution probabilities. a) One-dimensional site-diluted long-range lattice with power-law interactions, system size N = 8192 and interaction exponent α = 1.4. (b) PW2 graph with N = 8192 and α = 1.4. (c) Graph with power-law spatially correlated disorder, where the bond are deleted with probability pi,j = 1 −C/rα i,j shown for C = 1 −p and α = 1.4.

Figure 5. (a) Schematic comparison of the two spectral gaps: the graph Laplacian gap δλ (blue line), setting the energy scale of the unperturbed Hamiltonian at the Heisenberg point, and the anisotropy-induced gap δε∆(red line). The critical point is determined by the condition δλ = δε∆c. (b) Solid lines represents the perturbation energy scale δε∆= s(1 −∆)degG as a function of 1 −∆for different bond activation probabil- ities C > Cp at fixed α = 1.8, where degG is the numerically computed degree averaged over the graph nodes.
